The maintenance of a stable internal environment in the body, which is supported by the coordinated activities of various organs.
An extinct hominid species that lived about 1.5 million years ago, possessing a brain capacity of around 900 cc.
The first human-like hominid, characterized by a brain capacity of 650-800 cc and a non-meat diet.
Modern humans who arose in Africa during the ice age and developed distinct cultures, agriculture, and language.
A state of chemical equilibrium in which all reactants and products are present in the same physical phase.
The physiological ability of warm-blooded animals to maintain a constant internal body temperature independent of environmental fluctuations.
Complexes classified by whether the central metal is bound to only one type of donor group (homoleptic) or to multiple different types of donor groups (heteroleptic).
Coordination compounds containing only carbonyl (CO) ligands bonded to a transition metal, exhibiting specific geometries like tetrahedral, trigonal bipyramidal, or octahedral.
A group or series of organic compounds that contain the same characteristic functional group, can be represented by a general molecular formula, and whose successive members differ by a -CH2- unit.
Anatomical similarities in structures of different organisms indicating shared common ancestry, where the same structure evolved along different directions due to adaptations.
A type of covalent bond fission where each of the bonded atoms retains one electron of the shared pair, forming neutral free radicals.
A polysaccharide that is composed of only one repeating type of monosaccharide unit.
The physical law stating that the restoring force exerted by an ideal spring is proportional to the displacement from its equilibrium position and acts in the opposite direction.
The total horizontal distance traveled by a projectile during its entire time of flight.
The positive feedback loop where the foetal ejection reflex stimulates oxytocin release, inducing stronger uterine contractions and further oxytocin secretion.
The increase of cardiac output stimulated by hormones released from the adrenal medulla.
Specific proteins located in target tissues that bind to hormones to form a hormone-receptor complex and trigger biochemical changes.
Non-nutrient chemicals produced in trace amounts that act as intercellular messengers to coordinate physiological functions.
The chronological sequence of anatomical and physiological milestones of the fetus during the nine-month gestation period.
The network of endocrine glands and hormone-producing tissues distributed throughout the body that secrete hormones directly into the circulatory system to regulate physiological functions.
The organ system in humans responsible for waste elimination, consisting of a pair of kidneys, a pair of ureters, a urinary bladder, and a urethra.
A major international scientific initiative launched in 1990 to determine the complete DNA sequence of the human genome and identify all its genes.
A retrovirus containing an RNA genome enclosed in an envelope that causes AIDS by targeting and destroying immune cells.
The anatomical pathway in humans starting from the nostrils, passing through the pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, and bronchioles.
The process during decomposition in soil that leads to the accumulation of humus, a dark-colored, amorphous, and highly resistant substance that serves as a nutrient reservoir.
An antibody-mediated acquired immune response occurring in the blood.
An antibody-mediated immune response promoted by the action of thymosins.
A rule stating that for degenerate orbitals, pairing of electrons does not begin until each orbital in the subshell is singly occupied with one electron.
A geometrical construction principle stating that every point on a wavefront acts as a source of secondary wavelets, and their forward envelope determines the new wavefront position.
A set of equivalent orbitals of equal energy and shape formed by the redistribution of energy during the hybridisation of atomic orbitals.
The process of intermixing atomic orbitals of slightly different energies within an atom to redistribute their energies, resulting in a new set of equivalent orbitals.
The mixing of atomic orbitals (such as sp3, dsp2, d2sp3, sp3d2) in coordination complexes to explain their spatial geometry and bonding.
The mixing of s and p atomic orbitals in carbon to form sp3, sp2, or sp hybrid orbitals, which determines the geometric shapes of organic molecules.
The addition of water to alkynes in the presence of mercuric sulphate and dilute sulphuric acid to form carbonyl compounds via isomerisation.
A braking system that uses fluid pressure to transmit equal retarding force from a master cylinder to the wheel cylinders of a vehicle.
A force-multiplying device consisting of two connected pistons of different cross-sectional areas, where a small input force on the smaller piston produces a large output force on the larger piston.
The uniform compressive stress exerted by a fluid on all sides of a body, and the resulting fractional change in its volume.
Compounds composed exclusively of carbon and hydrogen, which serve as vital sources of energy and raw materials for polymers.
The attractive force which binds a hydrogen atom covalently linked to a highly electronegative atom with another electronegative atom of the same or a different molecule.
A type of attractive interaction between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom and another electronegative atom.
A vision of using hydrogen as a renewable, non-polluting source of energy produced by splitting water and utilized via fuel cells.
Single-electron ions such as He+, Li2+, and Be3+ to which Bohr's atomic model can be successfully applied by incorporating the atomic number Z.
A type of fuel cell that utilizes the reaction between hydrogen and oxygen gases at porous carbon electrodes to produce electricity and water.
Distinct series of spectral lines characteristic of atomic hydrogen, including the Lyman, Balmer, and Paschen series.
Atoms consisting of a nucleus with positive charge +Ze and a single orbiting electron.
Enzymes that catalyze the cleavage of chemical bonds through the addition of water (hydrolysis).

canonical_label: Hydrolysis of Salts of Strong Acid and Weak Base
definition: The hydrolysis of the cation of a salt derived from a strong acid and a weak base, which produces hydronium ions and results in an acidic solution (pH < 7).

canonical_label: Hydrolysis of Salts of Weak Acid and Strong Base
definition: The hydrolysis of the anion of a salt derived from a weak acid and a strong base, which produces hydroxide ions and results in an alkaline solution (pH > 7).

canonical_label: Hydrolysis of Salts of Weak Acid and Weak Base
definition: The simultaneous hydrolysis of both the cation and anion of a salt derived from a weak acid and a weak base, where the pH is determined by the relative values of pKa and pKb.
A trigonal pyramidal ion (H3O+) formed when a highly reactive bare proton (H+) binds to the lone pair of a water molecule.
The phenomenon where liquid pressure at a given depth is independent of the shape or cross-sectional area of the container, meaning pressure is identical at all points at the same horizontal level.
A hormone that functions to raise the concentration of calcium ions in the blood.
A permanent stabilizing interaction involving the delocalization of sigma electrons of a C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an unsaturated system or an atom with an unshared p orbital.
The delocalization of electrons in alkenes involving the overlap of C-H sigma bonds with the adjacent pi system.
The stabilization of carbocations through the overlap of adjacent C-H sigma bonds with the empty p orbital of the positively charged carbon, dispersing the positive charge.
A clinical state where blood pressure is consistently higher than the normal range of 120/80 mmHg, potentially damaging vital organs.
A condition characterized by abnormally high rates of synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, which can lead to Graves' disease.
The outer zone of the cortex in a dicot stem, composed of collenchymatous cells that provide mechanical strength.
A region located at the base of the thalamus that regulates homeostatic functions like body temperature, hunger, thirst, and secretes hypothalamic hormones.

The scientific explanations for high tropical biodiversity, attributing it to long undisturbed evolutionary time, constant and predictable environments promoting niche specialization, and high solar energy driving productivity.

canonical_label: Hypothetical Monopole Modification of Gauss's Law
definition: The theoretical modification of Gauss's law for magnetism if magnetic monopoles existed, relating net magnetic flux to the enclosed magnetic charge.
A condition of underactive thyroid hormone synthesis, often caused by iodine deficiency, leading to goitre, cretinism, or irregular menstrual cycles.
A beam cross-section designed to reduce weight and prevent buckling while maintaining a large load-bearing surface and depth.
The equation of state for an ideal gas, PV = \mu RT, which relates pressure, volume, temperature, and the number of moles of a gas.

canonical_label: Ideal Gas Equation and Real Gas Behavior
definition: The equation of state relating pressure, volume, and absolute temperature of an ideal gas, which real gases approximate only at low pressures and high temperatures.
A solution that obeys Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration, characterized by zero enthalpy of mixing and zero volume change on mixing.
The chemical differentiation and identification of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines using specific reagents such as p-toluenesulphonyl chloride.

canonical_label: Immediate Health Consequences of Substance Abuse
definition: The immediate adverse physical and behavioral effects of excessive drug or alcohol intake, which can range from reckless behavior to fatal overdosing, coma, or organ failure.
The overall ability of the host organism to defend itself and fight off disease-causing agents, categorized into innate and acquired immunity.
The perpendicular distance of the initial velocity vector of the alpha-particle from the center of the nucleus.
The total effective opposition offered by a series combination of resistance, inductance, and capacitance to the flow of alternating current.

canonical_label: Impedance and Phase Angle in AC Circuits

definition: The total effective opposition offered by an AC circuit to the flow of alternating current, combining resistance, capacitive reactance, and inductive reactance.
The process by which the blastocyst attaches to and becomes embedded in the endometrium of the uterus, leading to pregnancy.
The product of a force and the short time duration over which it acts, which is equivalent to the total change in momentum produced.
A conservation method that involves protecting and conserving an entire ecosystem in its natural location to safeguard its biodiversity at all levels.
A process where fertilisation of an ovum by a sperm occurs outside the body in simulated laboratory conditions, often followed by embryo transfer.
The reduction in biological fitness and vigor resulting from continued self-pollination in hermaphrodite plants.
Non-membrane-bound structures lying free in the cytoplasm of prokaryotic cells that store reserve materials.
The superposition of waves from sources with rapidly changing phase differences, resulting in a simple addition of individual intensities.
A form of inheritance in which the heterozygous F1 generation exhibits a phenotype that is intermediate between the phenotypes of the two homozygous parents.
The 1:2:1 ratio of phenotypes (e.g., Red:Pink:White) observed in the F2 generation of a cross involving incomplete dominance, which matches the genotypic ratio.
A circulatory pattern in amphibians and reptiles with a three-chambered heart where oxygenated and deoxygenated blood mix in a single ventricle.
A chemical state where the central atom of a stable molecule has fewer than eight valence electrons.
A subset of sexually transmitted infections, specifically hepatitis-B, genital herpes, and HIV, which cannot currently be cured.
The physical principle stating that the conditions for zero net external force and zero net external torque on a system are independent of each other.
The principle that motion in a plane can be treated as two separate, simultaneous one-dimensional motions with constant acceleration along perpendicular directions.
The independent alignment and segregation of different chromosome pairs during meiosis I, providing the physical basis for Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment.

The process during meiosis where the segregation of alleles of one gene (e.g., R and r) occurs independently of the segregation of alleles of another gene (e.g., Y and y), producing four gamete genotypes in equal proportions.
The principle that different pairs of alleles segregate independently of one another during gamete formation, resulting in independent inheritance of traits.
The capacity of plants for unlimited, lifelong growth due to the persistent activity of active dividing cells.
A legislative response by the Indian Parliament, specifically its second amendment, designed to address biopiracy, patent terms, emergency provisions, and research initiatives.
The physiological impacts of water deficit on a plant, such as stomatal closure and leaf wilting, which indirectly limit the rate of photosynthesis.
A temporary dipole moment created in non-polar molecules when an external electric field displaces their positive and negative charge centers.

canonical_label: Induced Electric Fields from Time-Varying Magnetic Fields
definition: The phenomenon where a changing magnetic field generates an electric field, which exerts forces on stationary charges to produce an induced emf.
The voltage generated in a coil or circuit due to a change in the magnetic flux passing through it over time.

canonical_label: Induced EMF in a Rotating Coil
definition: The electromotive force induced in a coil rotating at a constant angular speed in a magnetic field, derived using Faraday's law of induction.
The phenomenon where an electromotive force is induced across the terminals of an open circuit despite the absence of a closed path for current flow.
Unbalanced bound charges that accumulate on the surfaces of a polarized dielectric, creating an internal field that opposes and reduces the external field.
A substrate (like lactose) that binds to and inactivates the repressor, allowing transcription of the operon to proceed.
The constant of proportionality relating the magnetic flux linkage through a coil to the current flowing through it.
The phenomenon where a momentary electric current is induced in a stationary coil by initiating or terminating the current in a neighboring stationary coil.
The permanent polarization of a σ-bond caused by the polarization of an adjacent σ-bond, which decreases rapidly with distance.
The opposition offered by an inductor to the flow of alternating current, proportional to both the AC frequency and the inductance.

canonical_label: Industrial and Construction Applications of d-Block Elements
definition: The utilization of transition metals and their alloys (like iron and steel) and compounds (like titanium oxide and manganese dioxide) in construction, pigments, and batteries.
The commercial and industrial uses of cellulose, obtained from wood and cotton, in manufacturing furniture, textiles, paper, and lacquers.
The utilization of coordination complexes in industrial processes, such as homogeneous catalysis, electroplating, and photography.

The practical application of Le Chatelier's principle in chemical manufacturing, such as continuously removing products to maintain a reaction quotient lower than the equilibrium constant, thereby driving the reaction forward.
The use of chemical principles to manufacture essential products such as fertilizers, acids, salts, polymers, metals, and alloys.
A classic observed case of natural selection in moth populations in England, where industrial pollution altered tree trunk coloration and shifted survival advantages between light and dark variants.
A collision in which total linear momentum is conserved, but some kinetic energy is lost to heat or deformation.
An electrode made of an unreactive metal (like platinum or gold) that does not participate in the chemical reaction but provides a surface for electron transfer and conduction.
The inherent property of a physical body to resist any change in its state of rest or uniform linear motion.
The three-step experimental procedure used to allow phage infection, strip viral coats from bacteria, and separate the bacterial cells from viral debris.
Diseases that are easily transmitted from one person to another, such as AIDS.
The inability of a couple to produce children despite unprotected sexual co-habitation, which can be caused by physical, congenital, immunological, or psychological factors.
The arrangement of flowers on the floral axis of a plant.
Electromagnetic radiation produced by hot bodies and molecular vibrations, often referred to as heat waves due to their ready absorption by water and other molecules.
The process by which characters are passed on from parent to progeny, serving as the fundamental basis of heredity.
The set of two independent physical parameters (such as position and velocity, amplitude and phase, or energy and phase) required to uniquely determine a linear simple harmonic motion.
Transient polypeptides (sigma and rho factors) that associate with bacterial RNA polymerase to alter its specificity for starting or ending transcription.
A non-specific type of defense present at birth that prevents the entry of foreign agents into the body using various barriers.
Classification of octahedral complexes based on whether they use inner (n-1)d orbitals (low spin) or outer nd orbitals (high spin) in hybridization.
The elements of the f-block, consisting of the lanthanoids (4f series) and actinoids (5f series), where f orbitals are progressively filled.
The release of semen by the penis into the vagina during copulation.

A selection technique where the insertion of foreign DNA within the coding sequence of a marker gene (like an antibiotic resistance gene or beta-galactosidase) inactivates that gene, allowing recombinants to be distinguished.
The active stage of breathing during which atmospheric air is drawn into the lungs due to an increase in thoracic volume and a decrease in intra-pulmonary pressure.
The extra volume of air that can be forcibly inhaled beyond a normal tidal inspiration.
The acceleration of an object at a specific instant in time, defined as the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero.
The limiting ratio of the net charge flowing across a cross-section to the time interval as the interval tends to zero.
The force acting on a body at any given instant depends only on the conditions at its location at that exact moment, without any memory or carry-over from its past motion.
The characteristic of the photoelectric effect where electron emission begins almost immediately (within 10^-9 seconds) after light of sufficient frequency strikes the surface.
The limiting value of average power as the time interval approaches zero, which can also be expressed as the dot product of force and velocity.
The rate of a chemical reaction at a specific moment in time, mathematically represented as the limit of the average rate as the time interval approaches zero.
The magnitude of the instantaneous velocity at any given instant.
The limit of the average velocity as the time interval becomes infinitesimally small, representing velocity at a specific instant.
Materials that offer high resistance to the passage of electricity and do not readily allow it to pass.
A peptide hormone secreted by beta-cells of the pancreas that lowers blood glucose levels by enhancing cellular glucose uptake and stimulating glycogenesis.
The post-translational processing of pro-insulin into active insulin, which involves the removal of the C peptide to leave the A and B chains linked.
The molecular structure of mature insulin, consisting of two short polypeptide chains (chain A and chain B) linked together by disulfide bridges.
The mathematical formulation of work done by a variable force as the definite integral of force with respect to displacement.
An equation derived by integrating the differential rate equation to establish a direct relationship between experimental concentration data at different times and the rate constant.

canonical_label: Integrated Rate Equation for First-Order Reactions
definition: The mathematical equation relating the rate constant, elapsed time, initial concentration, and remaining concentration of a first-order reactant.

canonical_label: Integrated Rate Equation for Zero Order Reaction
definition: The mathematical equation obtained by integrating the differential rate equation for a zero order reaction, expressed as [R] = -kt + [R]_0.

canonical_label: Integrated Rate Law for First-Order Reactions
Integrated Rate Law for Zero-Order Reactions
Intensive and Extensive Properties of Cells
definition: The distinction where cell potential is an intensive property independent of the amount of substance, while Gibbs energy is an extensive property depending on the number of electrons transferred.
A thermodynamic property that is independent of the amount of matter present in the system.
Thermodynamic state variables whose values are independent of the size or mass of the system, such as pressure, temperature, and density.
The forces acting between atoms, characterized by long-range attraction and short-range repulsion, which determine the physical state and spacing of matter.
The alternating bright and dark bands of light observed on a screen as a result of the interference of coherent light waves.
The conceptual distinction where interference typically involves superposition from a few discrete sources, while diffraction involves superposition from a continuous or large number of sources.

A theory stating that a catalyst participates in a reaction by forming temporary bonds with reactants to create a transitory intermediate complex, which then decomposes to yield products and regenerate the catalyst.
Tension-bearing protein fibers that are components of the cytoskeleton, helping to maintain cell shape.
A hydrogen bond formed between two different molecules, which can be of the same or different chemical compounds.

The electrostatic attraction between the nitrogen atom of one amine molecule and the hydrogen atom bonded to nitrogen of another amine molecule, which is strongest in primary amines and absent in tertiary amines.
The internal organization of the kidney, characterized by an outer cortex, an inner medulla divided into medullary pyramids, and the renal pelvis.

canonical_label: Internal and External Forces in a System
definition: Forces acting on particles within a system, categorized into internal forces (exerted by particles on each other, which cancel out in pairs) and external forces (exerted by bodies outside the system).
The chaotic, non-ordered motion of molecules within a body that characterizes its thermal state, as opposed to bulk ordered motion.
The sum of the kinetic and potential energies of the constituent molecules of a system, excluding bulk motion.

canonical_label: Internal Energy Change in Phase Transition
definition: The application of the First Law of Thermodynamics to determine how internal energy changes when a substance undergoes a phase change, such as vaporization.
Plant-specific characteristics, such as leaf anatomy, mesophyll cells, chloroplasts, chlorophyll content, and internal carbon dioxide concentration, that influence photosynthetic rate.
Forces exerted by the constituent parts of a system on each other, which cancel out in pairs when considering the system as a whole.
The finite resistance offered by the electrolyte of a cell to the flow of electric current.
Global agreements and summits, such as the Earth Summit and World Summit, designed to foster international cooperation for biodiversity conservation.
The internationally accepted decimal system of units for measurement, developed by the BIPM, which simplifies conversions and standardizes scientific communication.
The portions or segments of a plant stem located between two consecutive nodes.
The preparatory phase of the cell cycle between two successive M phases, during which the cell undergoes growth and DNA replication.
An interaction where the fitness of one species is significantly reduced in the presence of another competing species, often over limiting resources.
Interactions that occur between populations of two different species, which can be beneficial, detrimental, or neutral to the organisms involved.
Non-stoichiometric compounds formed when small atoms like hydrogen, carbon, or nitrogen are trapped inside the crystal lattices of transition metals.
The pressure of air within the lungs that determines the direction of air movement relative to atmospheric pressure.

Devices inserted into the uterus by medical professionals that prevent conception by increasing sperm phagocytosis, releasing copper ions to suppress sperm motility, or releasing hormones to prevent implantation.
Receptors located inside the target cell, often in the nucleus, that bind to lipid-soluble hormones to directly regulate gene expression and chromosome function.
A hydrogen bond formed when a hydrogen atom is attracted to an electronegative atom located within the same molecule.
The heightened risk of contracting chronic, blood-borne infections like AIDS and Hepatitis B through the sharing of infected needles and syringes.
Pure semiconductors where charge carriers are thermally generated, and doped semiconductors (n-type and p-type) where carrier concentration is altered by impurities.
The equilibrium concentration of charge carriers (electrons or holes) in an intrinsic semiconductor, denoted by n_i.
A pure semiconductor material, such as Silicon or Germanium, in which the concentration of conduction electrons is equal to the concentration of holes.
The property of electric charge remaining constant across different frames of reference in relative motion and under rotation.
A chemical bond formed through the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions (cations and anions).

manual_merge.reason: Manual high-precision merge: Ionic Compound == Ionic Compounds (plural_or_trivial_wording).

definition: The conductance of electricity by ions present in electrolytic solutions, which depends on the nature of the electrolyte, ion size, solvent viscosity, concentration, and temperature.
A type of equilibrium that specifically involves ions in aqueous solutions.
The equilibrium constant ($K_w$) representing the self-ionization of water, defined as the product of the molar concentrations of hydrogen and hydroxyl ions.
The energy required to remove an electron from actinoid atoms, which is lower than that of lanthanoids due to less penetration and better shielding of 5f orbitals.
The process in which a neutral molecule splits into charged ions upon dissolution.

canonical_label: Ionization Constant of Weak Acids and Bases

definition: The minimum energy required to completely free an electron from its ground state in an atom.
The energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from an isolated gaseous atom in its ground state.
The endothermic energy change associated with removing an electron from a gaseous atom to form a positive ion (cation) during ionic bond formation.

canonical_label: Ionization of Acids and Bases in Aqueous Medium
definition: The process by which acids and bases dissociate into their constituent ions in water, classified as strong or weak depending on the extent of dissociation.
A thermodynamic process that cannot be reversed to restore both the system and its surroundings to their original states.
The endocrine component of the pancreas, consisting of alpha-cells and beta-cells that secrete glucagon and insulin respectively.
A thermodynamic process that occurs at constant pressure, where the heat absorbed is used to change internal energy and perform work.
Atoms of different elements that possess the same mass number but different atomic numbers.
Classification of nuclides where isobars share the same mass number (A) but different atomic numbers (Z), and isotones share the same neutron number (N) but different atomic numbers (Z).
The internal structure of a monocotyledonous leaf characterized by stomata on both epidermal surfaces and an undifferentiated, uniform mesophyll.
A thermodynamic process that occurs at constant volume, resulting in zero work done by or on the system.
Enzymes that catalyze the structural rearrangement of isomers, including optical, geometric, or positional isomers.
Chemical processes where n-alkanes are converted into branched-chain isomers or dehydrogenated and cyclised into aromatic compounds like benzene.
The phenomenon of existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties.
The occurrence of coordination compounds with the same chemical formula but different structural arrangements or spatial orientations, including geometrical, optical, and ionisation isomerism.
Compounds that possess the same molecular formula but have different properties.
The curve representing the relationship between pressure and volume of a thermodynamic system at a constant temperature.
An expansion or compression of a gas at constant temperature against a constant external pressure.
A thermodynamic process in which the temperature of the system remains constant, meaning there is no change in internal energy and the heat supplied equals the work done.

The mechanism by which heat exchange occurs in an isothermal quasi-static process due to an infinitesimal temperature difference between the system and its surrounding reservoir, maintaining a nearly constant temperature.
The classification of solutions based on their relative osmotic pressures across a semipermeable membrane.
An experimental technique using isotopes to prove the dynamic nature of chemical equilibrium by demonstrating continuous atomic exchange despite constant macroscopic concentrations.
Atoms of the same element that share the same atomic number but have different mass numbers due to varying numbers of neutrons.
The property that fluid pressure at a point in a fluid at rest is equal in all directions, making it a scalar quantity.

canonical_label: IUCN Red List and Extinction Trends
definition: The documentation of recent species extinctions and current extinction threats across various taxonomic groups.
The systematic additive nomenclature system used to name coordination compounds, specifying the order of cations, ligands, and the central metal.

canonical_label: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alkanes and Alkenes

definition: The systematic rules, including the lowest sum rule and alphabetical ordering of substituents, used to assign standardized names to branched-chain alkanes.

canonical_label: IUPAC Nomenclature of Carbonyl and Carboxyl Compounds
definition: The systematic naming and structural representation of aldehydes, ketones, and carboxylic acids based on IUPAC rules.
The systematic naming and formulation of coordination complexes according to IUPAC rules.

canonical_label: IUPAC Nomenclature of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
definition: The systematic method of naming halogenated organic compounds where halogens are treated as substituents on the parent hydrocarbon chain.
The systematic naming of halogenated alkenes, prioritizing the double bond over the halogen substituent for numbering the carbon chain.
The systematic rules established by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry to name organic compounds based on their structure and functional groups.
A systematic method of naming chemical compounds where names are directly correlated with their chemical structure, consisting of a parent chain, prefixes, and suffixes.

canonical_label: IUPAC Rules for Branched Chain Alkanes
definition: The systematic rules used to name branched alkanes, including identifying the longest carbon chain, numbering to give substituents the lowest locants, and listing substituents alphabetically.
A standardized naming system established by IUPAC for elements with atomic numbers greater than 100, using numerical roots derived from the digits of the atomic number followed by the suffix '-ium'.
The initial phase of the cardiac cycle where all four chambers of the heart are in a relaxed state, allowing blood to flow into the ventricles.
Limbs with joints that characterize the phylum Arthropoda, allowing for specialized movement.
Points of contact between bones, or between bones and cartilages, that act as fulcrums to facilitate movement using force generated by muscles.
The process by which the passage of an electric current through a conductor releases heat due to collisions between charge carriers and atoms.
The application of an external voltage to a p-n junction, either in forward bias (reducing the barrier) or reverse bias (increasing the barrier).
The phenomenon where the reverse current increases sharply when the reverse bias voltage reaches a critical threshold called the breakdown voltage.
Applying Kirchhoff's first rule at circuit junctions to systematically assign and minimize the number of unknown branch currents.
A specialized cellular region formed where the distal convoluted tubule meets the afferent arteriole, which regulates GFR by releasing renin.
The process of nuclear division in mitosis, divided into four progressive stages: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase.

A cyclic structure of benzene consisting of six carbon atoms with alternating single and double bonds, where the bonds oscillate rapidly to explain the existence of only one ortho-disubstituted isomer.

An formulation of the Second Law of Thermodynamics stating that it is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work.
An absolute temperature scale with its zero point at absolute zero, where the unit size is equal to the Celsius degree.

canonical_label: Kepler's Law of Periods for Satellites
definition: The relationship showing that the square of a satellite's orbital period is proportional to the cube of its orbital radius.
The physical basis of Kepler's second law in angular momentum conservation, and the universality of Kepler's third law constant for bodies orbiting the same central mass.
Three laws extracted by Johannes Kepler from Tycho Brahe's observational data that describe the motion of planets and laid the foundation for Newton's law of gravitation.
Crucial species that drive major ecosystem functions, whose extinction poses a severe threat to the stability and safety of the entire ecosystem.
The ultimate clinical method to correct acute renal failure by transplanting a functioning kidney from a compatible donor.
A unit of energy equivalent to the energy consumed by a 1000-watt appliance in one hour.
The set of equations that describe the relationship between angular displacement, initial and final angular velocities, uniform angular acceleration, and time.

canonical_label: Kinematic Equations for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
definition: A set of three fundamental equations relating displacement, time, initial and final velocity, and constant acceleration for rectilinear motion.
The restriction that standard kinematic equations for constant acceleration do not apply to uniform circular motion because the acceleration vector's direction changes.
The analysis and identification of physically possible or impossible motion graphs (such as position-time, velocity-time, and acceleration-time plots) and mapping them to physical scenarios.
The branch of physics that describes the motion of objects without considering the forces or causes that produce the motion.

The derivation showing that the total pressure of a mixture of non-reactive ideal gases is the sum of their individual partial pressures, based on equal average kinetic energies in thermal equilibrium.
A scalar quantity defined as half the product of an object's mass and the square of its speed, representing the energy of motion.

canonical_label: Kinetic Energy Decomposition of a System
definition: The separation of a system's total kinetic energy into the translational kinetic energy of its centre of mass and the kinetic energy of motion relative to the centre of mass.

canonical_label: Kinetic Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion
definition: The energy possessed by an oscillating particle due to its motion, which varies periodically with time and peaks at the mean position.

canonical_label: Kinetic Energy of an Orbiting Satellite
definition: The energy possessed by a satellite due to its motion in a circular orbit, which is positive and equal to half the magnitude of the potential energy.
The kinetic energy of an electron in a hydrogen atom, expressed in terms of its orbital radius and charge.
The frictional force that opposes relative motion between surfaces in contact once relative motion has commenced.
The physical interpretation showing that the absolute temperature of a gas is directly proportional to the average translational kinetic energy of its molecules.
A physical theory that explains the macroscopic behavior of gases by assuming they are composed of rapidly moving atoms or molecules with negligible inter-atomic forces.
The foundational molecular model of a gas, viewing it as a large collection of molecules in continuous random motion undergoing elastic collisions with negligible intermolecular forces.
The highest taxonomic category in the biological classification system, grouping related phyla or divisions.
The taxonomic kingdom that comprises all animals belonging to various phyla.
A distinct kingdom established for heterotrophic organisms with chitinous cell walls, separating them from autotrophic plants.
A unique kingdom of heterotrophic, eukaryotic organisms characterized by diverse habitats, chitinous cell walls, and absorptive nutrition.
The kingdom in the five-kingdom system that encompasses all prokaryotic organisms.
The taxonomic kingdom that comprises all plants from various divisions.
The kingdom in the five-kingdom system that groups all unicellular eukaryotic organisms.
A rule based on the conservation of charge stating that the sum of currents entering a junction equals the sum of currents leaving it, independent of wire geometry.
Two fundamental rules used to analyze complex electrical circuits: the junction rule based on charge conservation and the loop rule based on energy conservation.

A quantitative method for estimating nitrogen where the organic compound is digested with concentrated sulphuric acid to form ammonium sulphate, which is then decomposed with alkali to release ammonia for back-titration.
A chromosomal disorder in males caused by an extra X chromosome (47, XXY), leading to sterile individuals with some feminized traits.
An experimental setup using potassium hydroxide (KOH) to absorb carbon dioxide, demonstrating that carbon dioxide is essential for photosynthesis.

aliases: Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions
canonical_label: Kohlrausch Law of Independent Migration of Ions
definition: A law stating that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the individual limiting molar conductivities of its constituent cations and anions.

manual_merge.reason: Manual high-precision merge: Kohlrausch Law of Independent Migration of Ions == Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions (plural_or_trivial_wording).

definition: An electrolytic method where aqueous solutions of alkali metal salts of carboxylic acids undergo decarboxylation at the anode to produce hydrocarbons containing twice the number of carbon atoms present in the alkyl group of the starting acid.
An electrolytic process where an aqueous solution of a sodium or potassium salt of a carboxylic acid yields an alkane with an even number of carbon atoms at the anode.
A specialized leaf anatomy in C4 plants characterized by a wreath-like arrangement of large bundle sheath cells around the vascular bundles.
The tricarboxylic acid pathway operating in the mitochondrial matrix that oxidizes acetyl CoA to generate carbon dioxide, NADH, and FADH2.
A transcriptionally regulated system in bacteria where a polycistronic structural gene is regulated by a common promoter and regulatory genes.
The process of milk production by the mammary glands, which undergo differentiation during pregnancy and begin secretion toward the end of the term.
Specialized lymphatic vessels located in the intestinal villi through which fats are absorbed into the lymph.
A group of beneficial bacteria, such as Lactobacillus, that convert milk into curd by producing acids that coagulate and partially digest milk proteins, while enriching it with Vitamin B12.
An anaerobic pathway in certain bacteria and animal muscle cells where pyruvic acid is reduced to lactic acid by lactate dehydrogenase.
A reducing disaccharide, commonly known as milk sugar, composed of beta-D-galactose and beta-D-glucose linked by a beta-1,4-glycosidic bond.
An early evolutionary hypothesis proposing that evolution is driven by the use and disuse of organs, with acquired characters passed to offspring.
The green, expanded blade of a leaf containing veins and veinlets that provide rigidity and transport channels.
A fluid flow regime where layers of liquid slide smoothly over one another.
Complex carbohydrates that serve as the primary stored food reserves in brown algae.
The regular decrease in atomic and ionic radii of the 5d transition elements due to the imperfect shielding of one 4f electron by another.
The series of fourteen elements following lanthanum in the f-block, characterized by the filling of the 4f subshell and a predominant +3 oxidation state.
Two series of 14 elements each from the sixth and seventh periods, placed in separate panels at the bottom of the periodic table to maintain symmetry.
The correction to Newton's formula for the speed of sound in gases, assuming that pressure variations occur adiabatically rather than isothermally.
A chemical test used to detect nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus by fusing the organic compound with sodium metal to convert covalent elements into water-soluble ionic forms.
The quantity of heat absorbed or released per unit mass of a substance during a phase change at constant temperature.
The ecological pattern where species richness and diversity are highest in the tropics near the equator and progressively decrease toward the polar regions.

canonical_label: Lattice and Solvation Enthalpy in Solubility
definition: The thermodynamic factors governing the solubility of ionic solids, where dissolution requires the solvation enthalpy to overcome the lattice enthalpy.
The energy released when gaseous ions pack together to form an ionic solid lattice, which serves as a qualitative measure of the compound's stability.
Kepler's second law, which states that the line joining a planet to the Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time.
The law stating that at a constant temperature, the ratio of the product of concentrations of products to reactants, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients, remains constant.

canonical_label: Law of Conservation of Linear Momentum for a System
definition: The principle that the total linear momentum of a system of particles remains constant if the net external force acting on the system is zero.
A fundamental law of chemistry stating that matter can neither be created nor destroyed, resulting in no net change in mass during physical and chemical changes.
The principle that the total momentum of an isolated system of interacting particles remains constant over time, regardless of the nature of their interactions.
A formula used to calculate the magnitude of the resultant vector of two vectors based on their individual magnitudes and the angle between them.
A fundamental chemical law stating that a given compound always contains exactly the same proportion of elements by weight, regardless of its source or method of preparation.

canonical_label: Law of Definite Proportions by Volume
definition: The formulation of the law of definite proportions applied specifically to the volumes of reacting gases.
A Mendelian principle stating that traits are controlled by paired factors, and in a dissimilar pair, one factor dominates and masks the expression of the other recessive factor.
A law stating that in thermal equilibrium, the total energy of a system is distributed equally among all possible energy modes, with each quadratic term contributing an average energy of 1/2 k_B T.
A Mendelian law stating that when two pairs of traits are combined in a hybrid, the segregation of one pair of characters is independent of the other pair of characters.
The principle that a body remains in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled by an external force to act otherwise.
The principle that the active mass (concentration) of reacting substances determines the relationship between reactants and products at equilibrium.

A chemical law stating that if two elements combine to form more than one compound, the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other element are in the ratio of small whole numbers.
A classification rule proposed by John Newlands stating that when elements are arranged in increasing order of atomic weights, every eighth element exhibits properties similar to the first.
Kepler's first law, which states that all planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun situated at one of the two foci.
Kepler's third law, which states that the square of the orbital period of a planet is directly proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its elliptical orbit.
A principle of inheritance stating that allele pairs segregate during gamete formation so that each gamete receives only one allele, with no blending of traits.
A trigonometric relationship relating the magnitudes of vectors to the sines of their opposite angles to determine the direction of the resultant vector.
The five fundamental laws governing how chemical substances combine, which form the basis of Dalton's atomic theory.

manual_merge.reason: Manual high-precision merge: Laws of Chemical Combination == Laws of Chemical Combinations (plural_or_trivial_wording).

definition: A fundamental principle stating that if a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure, the system will adjust its equilibrium position to counteract and minimize the effect of that change.
The rule stating that an increase in pressure on a gaseous equilibrium system shifts the equilibrium in the direction that decreases the total number of moles of gas.
A widely used secondary battery consisting of a lead anode, a lead dioxide cathode, and a 38% sulphuric acid electrolyte.
The chemical reactions occurring at the anode and cathode during the discharging (use) and charging (reversal) of a lead storage battery.
A lateral, generally flattened vegetative organ borne on the stem that originates from shoot apical meristems and is primary for photosynthesis.
The basal part of a leaf that attaches it to the stem, which may feature stipules, a sheath in monocots, or a swollen pulvinus in legumes.
The loss of turgidity in leaves due to water stress, resulting in reduced surface area for light absorption and decreased metabolic activity.
The second stage of the moss gametophyte, which develops from the secondary protonema as a lateral bud, consisting of upright, slender axes with spirally arranged leaves.

canonical_label: Lens Systems and Effective Focal Length
definition: The behavior and mathematical analysis of combinations of multiple lenses, where the image of the first lens acts as the object for the subsequent lens.
A law stating that the polarity of an induced electromotive force (emf) is such that it produces a current opposing the change in magnetic flux that produced it.
Colorless plastids that store nutrients, further classified into amyloplasts (storing starch), elaioplasts (storing oils/fats), and aleuroplasts (storing proteins).
The categorization of animal body plans based on the complexity of cellular arrangement, ranging from cellular, tissue, organ, to organ system levels.
A simple machine consisting of a light rod pivoted at a fixed point, used to lift loads by applying effort at another point.
A chemical species that acts as an electron-pair acceptor.
A chemical species that acts as an electron-pair donor.

manual_merge.reason: Manual high-precision merge: Lewis Base == Lewis Bases (plural_or_trivial_wording).

definition: A representation of molecules that uses dots to depict valence electrons and shared electron pairs between combining atoms.
A visual representation of bonding in molecules and polyatomic ions showing shared electron pairs, lone pairs, and the octet status of constituent atoms.
Cells present in the intertubular spaces of the testis that produce androgen hormones, primarily testosterone.
Symbiotic, mutually beneficial associations between an autotrophic algal component (phycobiont) and a heterotrophic fungal component (mycobiont).
Characteristics of an organism's life cycle, such as survival and reproduction, that evolve in response to the abiotic and biotic constraints of their habitat.
Ions or molecules bound to the central metal atom in a coordination entity, classified as unidentate, didentate, or polydentate based on the number of donor atoms they use to bind.
Enzymes that catalyze the joining of two molecules, coupled with the breakdown of a pyrophosphate bond.
The process of mixing the cut gene of interest and cut vector DNA with DNA ligase to join them together, producing recombinant DNA.
The discovery by Jan Ingenhousz that sunlight and green plant parts are essential for oxygen release and air purification.
Complexes composed of hundreds of pigment molecules bound to proteins (also called antennae) that absorb various wavelengths of light to optimize photosynthetic efficiency.

canonical_label: Light Intensity and Photosynthesis Rate Relationship
definition: The relationship where the rate of photosynthesis increases linearly with incident light at low intensities, but levels off at higher intensities as other factors become limiting.
The photochemical phase of photosynthesis that includes light absorption, water splitting, oxygen release, and the formation of high-energy chemical intermediates like ATP and NADPH.

manual_merge.reason: Manual high-precision merge: Light Reaction == Light Reactions (plural_or_trivial_wording).

definition: The specific light intensity, occurring at about 10 percent of full sunlight, beyond which further increases in light do not increase the rate of photosynthesis.
A complex network of deep brain structures, including the amygdala and hippocampus, involved in emotion, motivation, memory, and autonomic responses.
The restrictions of the Arrhenius definition, which is confined to aqueous solutions and cannot account for the basicity of substances like ammonia (NH3) that do not contain hydroxyl groups.
The physical constraints under which Bernoulli's equation is valid, specifically requiring non-viscous, incompressible, and steady flow.
The specific shortcomings of Bohr's atomic theory, including its failure to explain multi-electron spectra, spectral line splitting, and chemical bonding.
The failures of Dalton's theory, specifically its inability to explain the laws of gaseous volumes and the underlying reasons for atomic combination.

The inherent constraints of using dimensional analysis to derive formulas, such as the inability to determine dimensionless constants or to distinguish between different physical quantities that share the same dimensions.

The limitation that while dimensional analysis can prove an equation wrong if its dimensions are inconsistent, it cannot guarantee that a dimensionally correct equation is physically correct due to the presence of dimensionless constants or functions.
The constraints of ecological pyramids, including the exclusion of saprophytes, assumption of simple food chains, and inability to account for species occupying multiple trophic levels.
The failure of Rutherford's classical model to explain atomic stability due to electromagnetic radiation of accelerating charges, and its inability to account for observed discrete line spectra.
The failure of the Bohr model to explain atomic structure due to its treatment of electrons solely as particles in fixed orbits, ignoring wave-particle duality and the uncertainty principle.
The theoretical shortcomings of the octet rule, including its failure to predict noble gas reactivity, molecular geometry, and relative thermodynamic stability.
The value that the molar conductivity of an electrolyte reaches as the concentration of the solution approaches zero (infinite dilution).
The reactant in a chemical reaction that is completely consumed first, thereby limiting the amount of product that can be formed.
The amount of electric charge per unit length along a one-dimensional line or wire.
An approximate method in wave mechanics where atomic orbitals are mathematically combined by addition or subtraction of their wave functions to represent molecular orbitals.
An oscillating system where the restoring force is linearly proportional to the displacement.
The mass per unit length of a string, representing its inertial property in wave propagation.
The product of a body's mass and its velocity, representing a vector quantity that indicates the quantity of motion.

canonical_label: Linear Momentum of a Single Particle

definition: An oscillating system consisting of a mass subject to a Hooke's law restoring force proportional to displacement.

canonical_label: Linear Relation between Stopping Potential and Frequency
definition: The linear dependence of the stopping potential on the frequency of incident radiation, characterized by a universal slope of h/e.
A transverse wave in which the displacement of each point is confined to a single direction perpendicular to the direction of propagation.
The physical association and co-inheritance of genes located on the same chromosome, which prevents them from assorting independently.
The basic structural framework of the cell membrane, composed of phospholipids arranged in two layers with polar heads facing outwards and hydrophobic tails facing inwards.
The phenomenon where lipids are found in the acid-insoluble fraction despite their low molecular weight, because they form water-insoluble vesicles when membranes are disrupted.
Water-insoluble organic molecules composed of fatty acids (saturated or unsaturated) and glycerol, which can form complex structures like triglycerides and phospholipids.
An equilibrium state in a closed system where the rate of evaporation of a liquid equals the rate of condensation of its vapour.
A group of bryophytes characterized by a thalloid or leafy plant body, growing in moist, shady habitats.
The perpendicular distances from the fulcrum to the lines of action of the load and effort forces, respectively.
Voluntary movements of an organism that result in a change of place or physical location.

canonical_label: Logarithmic Relationship between pKa and pKb
definition: The additive relationship between the negative logarithms of the ionization constants of a conjugate pair, expressed as pKa + pKb = pKw, which equals 14 at 298K.
The reduction of bond angles and distortion of ideal molecular geometries caused by the greater repulsive force exerted by lone pairs compared to bonding pairs in hybridised orbitals.
The application of stepping up voltage to high values to minimize Joule heating losses during the transmission of electrical energy over long distances.

canonical_label: Long Form of the Periodic Table
definition: The modern arrangement of elements in horizontal rows (periods) and vertical columns (groups) based on atomic numbers and electronic configurations.
The approximation used when the length of a solenoid is much larger than its radius, allowing the use of the ideal solenoid formula.
The stress and fractional change in length that occur when a body is stretched or compressed by forces acting normal to its cross-sectional area.
A wave in which the constituents of the medium oscillate parallel to (along) the direction of wave propagation.
The mathematical representation of longitudinal wave displacement, where the displacement of elements is parallel to the direction of wave propagation.
The propagation of longitudinal waves, which requires a bulk modulus of elasticity and can occur in solids, liquids, and gases.
Applying Kirchhoff's second rule around closed loops to generate independent algebraic equations for solving unknown currents.
The total force experienced by a moving point charge in the presence of both electric and magnetic fields.

canonical_label: Lorentz Force Explanation of Motional EMF
definition: An explanation of motional emf derived from the magnetic Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers within a moving conductor.
The derivation and explanation of motional electromotive force based on the magnetic Lorentz force acting on moving charges within a conductor.
An arrangement of elements based on plotting physical properties against atomic weight, which revealed a periodically repeating pattern of varying lengths.
The reduction in the vapour pressure of a solvent when a non-volatile solute is dissolved in it, caused by solute particles occupying surface area.
The phase following ovulation where the remaining parts of the Graafian follicle transform into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone to maintain the endometrium.
Enzymes that catalyze the removal of groups from substrates by mechanisms other than hydrolysis, typically forming double bonds.
A colourless liquid within the lymphatic system containing specialized lymphocytes and serving as a transport medium for nutrients, hormones, and fats.
A specialized network of vessels that collects interstitial fluid and returns it to the major veins.
The specialized organs of the immune system where lymphocytes originate, mature, and proliferate, classified into primary and secondary lymphoid organs.
Membrane-bound vesicular organelles formed by the Golgi apparatus that contain acidic hydrolytic enzymes capable of digesting macromolecules.
Measurable physical quantities of a bulk system, such as pressure, volume, temperature, mass, and composition.
The enhancement of magnetic interaction and induced current between coils by inserting a high-permeability material, such as an iron rod, along their common axis.

canonical_label: Magnetic Dipole in a Magnetic Field
definition: The behavior of a magnetic dipole (such as a bar magnet) in a uniform magnetic field, characterized by zero net force, a torque given by the cross product of the dipole moment and the magnetic field, and potential energy given by their negative dot product.

canonical_label: Magnetic Dipole in a Uniform Magnetic Field
definition: The behavior of a magnetic dipole placed in a uniform magnetic field, where it experiences a restoring torque that attempts to align its magnetic moment with the external field.
A vector quantity representing the magnetic strength and orientation of a current loop, defined as the product of current and the area vector.

canonical_label: Magnetic Dipole Moment of a Current Loop
definition: The product of the number of turns, current, and area vector of a planar current-carrying loop.
The magnetic energy stored per unit volume in a magnetic field, proportional to the square of the magnetic field strength.
A vector field produced by moving charges or currents, defined at each point in space, which obeys the principle of superposition.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field at the Center of a Circular Loop
definition: The magnetic field at the exact center of a circular current-carrying loop, which is a special case of the axial field formula where the distance along the axis is zero.
Continuous closed loops that provide a visual representation of a magnetic field, where the tangent at any point indicates the field direction and the line density represents field strength.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field of a Circular Loop
Magnetic Field of a Finite Solenoid
definition: The magnetic field pattern of a finite solenoid, which is strong, uniform, and axial inside, but weak and axial outside.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field of a Multi-turn Coil
definition: The total magnetic field produced at the center of a tightly wound coil with multiple turns, which is equal to the field of a single turn multiplied by the number of turns.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field of a Semi-circular Arc
definition: The magnetic field at the center of curvature of a semi-circular current-carrying wire, which is exactly half the magnitude of the field of a full circular loop.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field of a Straight Current-Carrying Wire
definition: The magnetic field lines generated by a straight current-carrying wire, which form concentric circles centered on the wire.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field of a Thick Wire
definition: The magnetic field distribution inside and outside a thick wire of radius a carrying a uniform current, where the field increases linearly with radius inside and decreases as 1/r outside.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field of an Ideal Solenoid
definition: The idealized magnetic field of an infinitely long solenoid, which is perfectly uniform inside and zero outside, given by B = μ_0 * n * I.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field of an Infinite Straight Wire
definition: The magnetic field produced at a radial distance r from an infinitely long straight wire carrying a steady current I, which is inversely proportional to the distance.

canonical_label: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Loop
definition: The magnetic field produced by a circular current-carrying loop at any point along its central axis, calculated by integrating the axial components of the fields from individual current elements.
The surface integral of the magnetic field over a given area, representing the total magnetic field passing through that surface.
The total magnetic flux calculated over a surface where the magnetic field varies, represented as the sum of the flux through all infinitesimal area elements.
The magnetic force exerted per unit length between two long, straight, parallel conductors carrying steady currents, given by the formula derived from Biot-Savart law and Lorentz force.

canonical_label: Magnetic Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor
definition: The macroscopic magnetic force experienced by a current-carrying wire in an external magnetic field, derived by summing the magnetic forces on individual moving charge carriers.

canonical_label: Magnetic Force on a Moving Charge
definition: The force component acting on a charge moving through a magnetic field, which depends on the charge, velocity, and magnetic field, acting perpendicular to both velocity and field.
An auxiliary vector field representing the contribution of external currents to the total magnetic field, independent of the material's magnetization.
Hypothetical isolated magnetic charges which are not known to exist, establishing the magnetic dipole as the most fundamental magnetic unit.
The magnetic behavior of a substance, classified as diamagnetic if all molecular orbitals are doubly occupied, or paramagnetic if one or more are singly occupied.
A quantum number designated by 'm_l' that describes the spatial orientation of an orbital relative to a set of coordinate axes.
The measure of the ability of a substance to support the formation of a magnetic field within itself, defined as the product of vacuum permeability and relative permeability.
The potential energy stored in a magnetic dipole due to its orientation relative to an external magnetic field, given by the dot product of the magnetic moment and the magnetic field vector.
The classification of coordination complexes as paramagnetic or diamagnetic based on the presence or absence of unpaired d-electrons, influenced by ligand strength.
The state in which all individual atomic magnetic dipoles in a paramagnetic material are perfectly aligned with the external magnetic field, preventing further increase in magnetisation.
A dimensionless parameter that measures how a material magnetizes in response to an applied external magnetic intensity.
The mathematical relationships and constants (magnetic susceptibility, permeability, and relative permeability) that characterize how a linear material responds to an applied magnetic field.

canonical_label: Magnetic Torque on a Current Loop

definition: The net magnetic moment per unit volume of a bulk material sample.

Quantities describing magnetic fields in materials: magnetic intensity (H) represents the external field contribution, while magnetisation (M) is the magnetic dipole moment per unit volume of the material.
A universal physical phenomenon involving magnetic fields, historically discovered in Magnesia and later linked to moving electric charges.
The ratio of the angular size of the image to the angular size of the object when placed at the near point, distinct from linear magnification.
The dynamic lift exerted on a spinning ball caused by a pressure difference resulting from asymmetric fluid velocity on opposite sides of the ball.
Glands including the seminal vesicles, prostate, and bulbourethral glands that secrete seminal plasma to nourish sperm and lubricate the penis.
A phenomenon where the male sex produces two different types of gametes with respect to sex chromosomes.
The production of two different types of gametes by human males, where 50 percent of sperms carry the X-chromosome and 50 percent carry the Y-chromosome.
The anatomical system in human males located in the pelvis region, consisting of the testes, accessory ducts, glands, and external genitalia.
The pathway of ducts, including the rete testis, vasa efferentia, epididymis, and vas deferens, that store and transport sperm.
The filtration unit of the nephron, also known as the renal corpuscle, which comprises the glomerulus and the enclosing Bowman's capsule.
The primary excretory organs of arthropods that remove nitrogenous wastes from the hemolymph.
The application of Thomas Malthus's ideas on population limits and resource competition to explain how natural selection favors individuals with adaptive heritable variations.
A reducing disaccharide composed of two alpha-D-glucose units linked by an alpha-1,4-glycosidic bond.

A law stating that the intensity of plane-polarised light transmitted through a polaroid varies as the square of the cosine of the angle between the light's polarisation direction and the polaroid's pass-axis.
A biological class that includes diverse orders such as Primata (monkeys, gorillas, gibbons) and Carnivora (tigers, cats, dogs).
Specialized milk-producing exocrine glands unique to mammals, used for the nourishment of their newborn offspring.
A soft, spongy layer of skin (mantle) covering the visceral hump of molluscs, creating a space (mantle cavity) that houses respiratory and excretory gills.
A rule stating that in the addition of hydrogen halides to unsymmetrical alkenes, the negative part of the addendum attaches to the double-bonded carbon with fewer hydrogen atoms.
A relation stating that under thermal equilibrium, the product of the free electron concentration and hole concentration is constant and equal to the square of the intrinsic carrier concentration.
Mass is the constant quantity of matter in an object, whereas weight is the variable force exerted on that mass by gravity.
The difference between the sum of the individual masses of a nucleus's constituent protons and neutrons and its actual measured nuclear mass.
The principle established by special relativity that mass is a form of energy, related by the equation E = mc^2.

canonical_label: Mass-energy equivalence of atomic mass unit
definition: The conversion factor relating unified atomic mass units (u) to energy in megaelectronvolts (MeV), where 1 u is equivalent to 931.5 MeV/c^2.
The mass of the solute divided by the total mass of the solution, multiplied by 100.
The mass of a component in a solution per 100 g of the solution.
Demographic indicators (MMR and IMR) used to measure the health status of mothers and infants, where a decrease indicates improved reproductive health.

canonical_label: Mathematical Analysis of Pressure Change via Reaction Quotient
definition: The quantitative determination of equilibrium shift direction by calculating the new reaction quotient (Q_c) after a volume/pressure change and comparing it to the equilibrium constant (K_c).
The mathematical formulation of the superposition of two harmonic waves of nearly equal frequencies to find the resultant displacement and amplitude modulation.
The sinusoidal wave equations that describe the spatial and temporal variation of the electric and magnetic field components of a plane electromagnetic wave.
The mathematical formulation of reflected waves traveling in the opposite direction, incorporating phase shifts depending on the boundary conditions.
The mathematical equation describing the projection of uniform circular motion on the x-axis, represented as a cosine function of time.
Anything which has mass and occupies space.
The wavelength of a matter wave, given by \lambda = h/p, which has physical significance.
Waves associated with the constituents of matter, such as subatomic particles and atoms, described within the framework of quantum mechanics.
The maximum current that can be drawn from a cell, occurring when the external resistance is zero.
The theoretical limit on mountain height on Earth determined by the elastic limit and shearing stress of rocks under gravity.
The maximum vertical displacement reached by a projectile, occurring at the point where its vertical velocity component becomes zero.
The kinetic energy of the fastest emitted photoelectrons, which is directly proportional to the stopping potential.
The maximum rate of an enzymatic reaction achieved when all available enzyme active sites are fully saturated with substrate molecules.
A statistical distribution showing the fraction of molecules in a reacting species possessing various kinetic energies at a given temperature.
A set of equations involving electric and magnetic fields, charge, and current densities that mathematically express all basic laws of electromagnetism.
The thermodynamic equation relating the molar specific heat capacity of an ideal gas at constant pressure and constant volume: Cp - Cv = R.
The average distance a gas molecule can travel between successive collisions with other molecules.
The simplified derivation of the mean free path formula assuming other molecules are at rest, yielding $l = 1/(n \pi d^2)$.
The ratio of the load force to the effort force in a machine, which determines the factor by which the machine multiplies the input force.
The ratio of the area of the larger output piston to the smaller input piston, representing the factor by which the input force is multiplied.

canonical_label: Mechanical Equilibrium of a Rigid Body
definition: A state in which a rigid body experiences no change in either its linear momentum or its angular momentum over time, requiring both net external force and net external torque to be zero.
Waves that require a material medium for propagation and depend on the elastic properties of the medium to transmit disturbances.
Heat transfer within a fluid characterized by the actual flow of matter due to temperature differences.

The three-step pathway of electrophilic aromatic substitution consisting of the generation of the electrophile, formation of the carbocation intermediate, and removal of a proton to restore aromaticity.
The process by which a disturbance is transmitted through a medium via local oscillations of constituent particles and restoring forces without bodily displacement of the medium.
The legal and intentional termination of an unwanted pregnancy under specific conditions such as rape, health risks, or casual relationships.
An Indian legal framework enacted to regulate safe abortions, reduce maternal mortality, and prevent illegal female foeticide by specifying strict conditions and required medical opinions.
The intentional or voluntary termination of a pregnancy before it reaches full term, also referred to as induced abortion.
The use of coordination complexes and chelating agents in medicine, particularly in chelate therapy for heavy metal poisoning and cancer treatment.

canonical_label: Medium Dependence of Mechanical Wave Speed
definition: The principle that the speed of a mechanical wave is determined by the medium's inertial and elastic properties.
The part of the hindbrain connected to the spinal cord that regulates respiration, cardiovascular reflexes, and gastric secretions.
An increasing osmolarity gradient from the renal cortex to the inner medulla, driven by NaCl and urea, which facilitates water reabsorption.
Radially arranged parenchymatous cells situated between the vascular bundles in a dicot stem.
A designation for countries possessing exceptionally high levels of biodiversity; India qualifies with 8.1% of global species diversity on 2.4% of the world's land area.
The structure in seed plants that contains the female gametophyte and is protected by integuments, attaching to the placenta via a funicle.
The process of formation of haploid megaspores from a diploid megaspore mother cell (MMC) through meiotic division.
A specialized cell division that reduces the chromosome number by half, resulting in the production of haploid daughter cells.
The first meiotic division characterized by homologous chromosome pairing, recombination, and separation into two cells.
The second meiotic division resembling mitosis, where sister chromatids separate to form four haploid cells.
The phenomenon of perfect diamagnetism in superconductors, where magnetic field lines are completely expelled from the material.
A hormone secreted by the pineal gland that regulates diurnal rhythms, sleep-wake cycles, body temperature, and influences metabolism and pigmentation.
Receptors located on the cell membrane of target cells that bind to extracellular hormones and trigger intracellular signaling without the hormone entering the cell.
Proteins associated with the cell membrane, classified as peripheral or integral based on their ease of extraction and location.
The onset of the first menstruation in human females, which begins at puberty.
The law stating that the physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic weights.

A tabular arrangement of elements in horizontal rows (series) and vertical columns (groups) in order of increasing atomic weights, which successfully predicted the existence and properties of undiscovered elements.
Genetic disorders mainly determined by the alteration or mutation of a single gene, transmitted to offspring according to Mendelian principles of inheritance.
The systematic cross-pollination experiments conducted by Mendel on garden peas to analyze the patterns of inheritance using statistical and mathematical tools.
The fundamental principles governing the transmission of genetic characters from parents to offspring, proposed by Gregor Mendel based on his hybridization experiments on garden peas.
The permanent cessation of menstrual cycles in human females, typically occurring around 50 years of age.
The reproductive cycle in female primates, repeated at an average interval of 28 to 29 days, starting from one menstruation to the next.
The initial phase of the menstrual cycle lasting 3 to 5 days, characterized by the breakdown of the endometrial lining and menstrual flow.
An instrument consisting of a long glass tube closed at one end, filled with mercury, and inverted into a trough of mercury, used to measure atmospheric pressure.
A primary battery suitable for low-current devices that maintains a constant cell potential of 1.35 V because its overall reaction does not involve ions in solution.
Mitotic divisions occurring in the apical and lateral cambium of plants that enable continuous growth throughout their life.
The initial phase of plant growth characterized by rapidly dividing cells at the root and shoot apices, featuring dense protoplasm, large nuclei, and thin cellulosic cell walls.
The monomeric subunit of a myosin filament, consisting of heavy meromyosin (HMM) and light meromyosin (LMM).

The classification of flowers based on the number of floral appendages (trimerous, tetramerous, or pentamerous) and the presence (bracteate) or absence (ebracteate) of reduced leaves at the base of the pedicel.
The landmark 1958 experiment using E. coli grown in 15N and 14N media that provided physical proof for the semiconservative replication of DNA.
The photosynthetic ground tissue of a leaf located between the upper and lower epidermis, composed of parenchyma cells containing chloroplasts.
Leaf cells containing a high concentration of chloroplasts, serving as the primary site for photosynthesis.
Specialized, differentiated infoldings of the cell membrane found in prokaryotic cells.
Substituents on a benzene ring that direct incoming electrophiles to the meta position, typically by withdrawing electron density from the ortho and para positions.
A multi-step sequence of linked chemical reactions occurring within a cell, where each individual step is catalyzed by a specific enzyme.
Biomolecules found in living organisms that are involved in metabolic processes.
The alternation of generations in certain cnidarians, where polyps produce medusae asexually and medusae produce polyps sexually.
A list of metals arranged in order of their relative tendency to release or lose electrons.
A bond formed by the back-donation of a pair of electrons from a filled d orbital of the metal into the vacant antibonding pi* orbital of carbon monoxide.
A covalent bond formed by the donation of a lone pair of electrons from the carbonyl carbon into a vacant orbital of the metal.
Inorganic ions required by certain enzymes that form coordination bonds with active site side chains and the substrate.
The phenomenon where zinc's completely filled 3d subshell prevents its d-electrons from participating in metallic bonding, unlike other 3d transition metals.
Elements bordering the diagonal line between metals and non-metals that exhibit properties characteristic of both classes.
The application of complex formation and decomposition in the extraction of precious metals and the purification of metals like nickel.
A body organization feature where the external and internal body is distinctly divided into repetitive segments or metameres.
A body plan characterized by external and internal segmentation with serial repetition of organs.
The second stage of mitosis where chromosomes complete condensation and align along the equatorial metaphase plate, attached to spindle fibres via kinetochores.
