The process of defining temperature scales on a thermometer using fixed reference points, such as the ice point and steam point of pure water.
The science and practice of measuring temperature using physical properties of substances that change predictably with temperature.
Nuclear fusion reactions that occur at extremely high temperatures, providing the nuclei with enough kinetic energy to overcome their mutual electrostatic repulsion.
An adsorption chromatography technique involving the separation of substances over a thin layer of an adsorbent coated on a glass plate.
A law stating that the entropy of a perfectly ordered, pure crystalline substance approaches zero as its temperature approaches absolute zero (0 K).
An early atomic model proposing that an atom is a positively charged sphere with electrons embedded within it to maintain electrostatic stability.
An early atomic model proposed by J.J. Thomson where positive charge is uniformly distributed throughout the atom's volume, with electrons embedded in it like seeds in a watermelon.
An anatomically air-tight chamber enclosing the lungs, bounded by the vertebral column, sternum, ribs, and diaphragm, essential for breathing.
A circulatory organ structure consisting of two auricles and one ventricle, characteristic of amphibians.
The extension of vector resolution to three dimensions, expressing a vector in terms of components along the x, y, and z axes.
The minimum energy that colliding molecules must possess for a collision to be effective, equal to the sum of activation energy and the initial energy of the reactants.
The characteristic minimum frequency of incident light below which the photoelectric effect is not observed for a given metal.
The minimum frequency of incident light required to cause photoelectric emission from a metal surface, below which no electrons are emitted.
Flattened membranous sacs organized in stacks (grana) within the chloroplast stroma, containing chlorophyll pigments.
Peptide hormones secreted by the thymus gland that stimulate the differentiation of T-lymphocytes and promote antibody production.
The age-related decline of the thymus gland, resulting in reduced thymosin production and a weakened immune system in older individuals.
A lobular endocrine gland located between the lungs and behind the sternum that plays a critical role in immune system development.
A protein hormone secreted by the thyroid gland that plays a key role in regulating blood calcium levels.
The anatomical structure of the thyroid gland, consisting of two lobes on either side of the trachea connected by the isthmus, composed of follicles and stromal tissues.
Hormones (tetraiodothyronine/thyroxine and triiodothyronine) synthesized by follicular cells of the thyroid gland that regulate basal metabolic rate and metabolism.
An iodinated derivative of the amino acid tyrosine produced by the thyroid gland, essential for preventing hypothyroidism and goitre.
A tumor-inducing plasmid from Agrobacterium tumefaciens that has been genetically modified (disarmed) to deliver genes of interest into plant cells without causing disease.
The volume of air inspired or expired during a normal, relaxed respiration, averaging about 500 mL in a healthy human.
The total time duration that a projectile remains in the air from the moment of projection until it returns to its initial launch height.

canonical_label: Time Period and Frequency in Circular Motion
definition: Time period is the duration of one complete revolution, while frequency is the number of complete revolutions completed per unit of time.
The total time taken by a satellite to complete one full revolution around the Earth.

canonical_label: Time Period of a Simple Pendulum
definition: The time required for a simple pendulum to complete one full oscillation, which depends only on the length of the pendulum and the acceleration due to gravity for small angles.
The induction of an electromotive force in stationary charges by a changing magnetic field, illustrating a relativistic symmetry in electromagnetism.
A group of similar cells along with intercellular substances that perform a specific function.
The regeneration of whole plants from plant parts (explants) grown in a sterile nutrient medium, utilizing the inherent capacity of a single cell to generate a whole plant (totipotency).
The fluid that leaks from blood capillaries into the spaces between cells, acting as a medium for exchange of nutrients and gases.
An abused substance containing the alkaloid nicotine, which stimulates adrenaline release, increases blood pressure and heart rate, and is linked to various cancers.
A traditional beverage common in southern India, produced by the fermentation of sap collected from palm trees.
The phenomenon where repeated use of drugs or alcohol increases the tolerance level of receptors in the body, requiring higher doses to achieve the same effect.
Oxidation reactions used to distinguish aldehydes from ketones, where aldehydes are easily oxidized to carboxylic acids by mild oxidizing agents.
The essential molecular and biological instruments required to perform genetic engineering, which include restriction enzymes, polymerase enzymes, ligases, vectors, and host organisms.
The rotational equivalent of linear force, defined as the vector product of the position vector and the force vector.
The equilibrium condition in a galvanometer where the deflecting magnetic torque is balanced by the restoring torsional torque of a spring.
The rotational analogue of force, mathematically defined as the vector product of the position vector of a particle and the force acting on it.
The rotational effect experienced by a current-carrying loop in a uniform magnetic field, which does not produce a net translational force.
The rotational force exerted on an electric dipole in an external electric field, mathematically represented as the cross product of the dipole moment and the electric field vector.
A law stating that the speed of efflux of a fluid from an open tank is equal to the speed a free-falling body would acquire in falling through the same vertical distance.
The weak repulsive interaction between adjacent bonds that hinders the free rotation about a single bond.
The total electric current flowing through an intrinsic semiconductor, which is the sum of the independent currents due to free electrons and holes.
The total electric flux through a large surface, calculated by summing or integrating the flux contributions of all individual infinitesimal area elements making up the surface.
The sum of the kinetic and potential energies of the orbiting electron, which is negative for a stable orbit.

canonical_label: Total Internal Reflection in Optical Fibers
definition: The phenomenon where light traveling from a denser to a rarer medium is completely reflected back at the interface if the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle, utilized in optical fibers.

canonical_label: Total Linear Momentum of a System of Particles
definition: The vector sum of the individual momenta of all particles in a system, which is equal to the product of the total mass of the system and the velocity of its centre of mass.
The sum of the kinetic energy and potential energy of a system, which remains constant in the presence of conservative forces.

canonical_label: Total Mechanical Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion
definition: The sum of the kinetic and potential energies of a harmonic oscillator, which remains constant and independent of time.

canonical_label: Total Mechanical Energy of an Orbiting Satellite
definition: The sum of the kinetic and potential energies of an orbiting satellite, which remains constant and negative for a closed orbit.
The process of summing the valence electrons of all combining atoms in a chemical species, adjusted by adding electrons for anions or subtracting electrons for cations.

canonical_label: Total Vapour Pressure of Binary Solutions
definition: The total pressure exerted by a mixture of volatile liquids, calculated as the sum of the partial pressures of its individual components.
The principle that an object's path of motion is determined by both its acceleration and its initial position and velocity.
The parabolic path followed by a projectile, mathematically described by expressing its vertical position as a quadratic function of its horizontal position.
The biological process of copying genetic information from a specific segment of one strand of DNA into a complementary strand of RNA.
A segment of DNA defined by a promoter, a structural gene, and a terminator that serves as the template for transcription.
Enzymes that catalyze the transfer of a functional group (other than hydrogen) from one substrate to another.
The process by which competent host cells take up foreign recombinant DNA from their surroundings, resulting in a change in their genetic characteristics.
A static electrical device designed to transfer electrical energy between circuits through electromagnetic induction, specifically to increase or decrease the voltage of an alternating current.
The physical mechanisms, such as flux leakage, winding resistance, eddy currents, and hysteresis, that cause energy dissipation in real-world transformers.
The mathematical relationship relating the primary and secondary voltages of a transformer to their respective number of turns.
Animals that have had their DNA manipulated to possess and express an extra (foreign) gene.
Specially created transgenic animals that serve as models for human diseases to facilitate the investigation of new treatments.
A temporary flow of electric charge that ceases once the driving electric field is neutralized by the accumulation of charges.
A definition classifying elements as transition metals if they possess a partially filled d-orbital in their ground state or in any of their common oxidation states.
Elements in Groups 3-12 characterized by the progressive filling of inner d orbitals, exhibiting metallic properties, high melting and boiling points, and strong interatomic bonding.
Metals which have an incomplete d subshell either in their neutral atom or in their common ionic states, according to the IUPAC definition.
An unstable, high-energy state that a substrate must pass through during its transformation into a product.
The shift from classical/Bohr atomic models to quantum mechanics to reconcile wave-particle duality and the uncertainty principle.
The process by which amino acids are polymerized to form a polypeptide chain, directed by the sequence of bases in mRNA.
Independent modes of motion associated with the linear movement of a molecule in space, each contributing 1/2 k_B T to the average energy.
The condition of a body where the vector sum of all external forces acting on it is zero.
The characteristic of electromagnetic waves where the oscillating electric and magnetic fields are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of wave propagation.
A wave in which the constituents of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation.
The propagation of transverse waves, which requires a medium with shear elasticity (typically solids) to support shear stress.
The periodic trend where metallic character increases down a group and decreases from left to right across a period.
A graphical method of adding two vectors by placing the tail of the second vector at the head of the first, where the resultant vector is drawn from the tail of the first to the head of the second.
A cyclic pathway in the mitochondrial matrix that oxidizes acetyl groups derived from pyruvic acid to produce carbon dioxide, NADH, FADH2, and ATP.
A genus of free-living fungi common in root ecosystems that acts as an effective biocontrol agent against several plant pathogens.
The cleavage of the six-carbon fructose 1,6-bisphosphate into two distinct three-carbon triose phosphate molecules: DHAP and PGAL.
A covalent bond formed when two atoms share three pairs of electrons.
The physical characteristics of a carbon-carbon triple bond, which is shorter and stronger than single and double carbon-carbon bonds.
A chemical bond consisting of one sigma bond formed by head-on orbital overlap and two pi bonds formed by lateral overlap of unhybridized p orbitals.
The fusion of a male gamete with two polar nuclei in the central cell to produce a triploid primary endosperm nucleus.
The unique temperature and pressure at which the solid, liquid, and vapour phases of a substance coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium.

canonical_label: Triple Point of Water as a Standard Fixed Point
definition: The unique temperature at which the solid, liquid, and gas phases of water coexist in equilibrium, chosen as the standard fixed point (273.16 K) for temperature scales.
A sequence of three nucleotides on mRNA that specifies a particular amino acid during protein synthesis.
Non-systematic names historically assigned to organic compounds based on their origin, properties, or historical significance.
An adapter RNA molecule that reads mRNA codons via its anticodon loop and carries the corresponding amino acid to the ribosome.
The specific position or feeding stage that an organism occupies in a food chain or community based on its nutritional source.

manual_merge.reason: Manual high-precision merge: Trophic Level == Trophic Levels (plural_or_trivial_wording).

definition: A regulatory fibrous protein that runs along the length of F-actin strands in muscle fibers.
A complex regulatory protein distributed at regular intervals on tropomyosin that masks myosin-binding sites in a resting muscle.
A plant line that has undergone continuous self-pollination and shows stable trait inheritance and expression for several generations.
The process by which nearly 99% of the glomerular filtrate is reclaimed back into the blood through active and passive transport mechanisms.
The active transport of substances such as hydrogen ions, potassium ions, and ammonia from the blood into the filtrate to maintain ionic and pH balance.

canonical_label: Tuning of Musical Instruments using Beats
definition: The practical application of adjusting the tension and frequency of an instrument's string until the beat frequency relative to a reference decreases to zero.
An unsteady fluid flow occurring beyond a critical speed, characterized by rapid fluctuations in velocity and whirlpool-like regions.
A chromosomal disorder in females caused by the absence of one X chromosome (45, X0), resulting in sterility and underdeveloped sexual characters.
A collision where the motion of the bodies occurs in a plane, requiring momentum conservation to be resolved along two perpendicular axes.
Uniform circular motion represented as a combination of two mutually perpendicular simple harmonic motions with a phase difference of pi/2.
An early system of classification developed during Linnaeus' time that divided all living organisms into the kingdoms Plantae and Animalia.
The classification of neurons into multipolar, bipolar, and unipolar types based on the number of axons and dendrites they possess.
The classification of redox reactions into four distinct categories: combination, decomposition, displacement, and disproportionation.
The three major classes of RNA molecules (mRNA, tRNA, and rRNA) that play distinct template, adapter, and structural/catalytic roles in protein synthesis.
The classification of systems into open, closed, or isolated based on their ability to exchange matter and energy with their surroundings.
Mobile electron carriers that transfer electrons between complexes in the inner mitochondrial membrane.
The maximum stress that a material can withstand before fracturing.
Electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths shorter than visible light, produced by very hot bodies like the Sun, and largely absorbed by the Earth's ozone layer.
The process of calculating currents and potential differences in a Wheatstone bridge circuit when the bridge is not balanced and current flows through the galvanometer arm.
The rule stating that when multiplying measured quantities, the relative error of the product is the sum of the relative errors of the individual multipliers.
Organisms consisting of a single cell that are capable of independent existence and performing all essential functions of life.
A state of rotational motion where the rate of change of angular velocity with respect to time remains constant.
The motion of an object traveling along a circular path at a constant speed, characterized by a centripetal acceleration directed toward the center.
The production of single-sex flowers on the same plant (monoecious) or different plants (dioecious) to prevent self-pollination.
A basic, arbitrarily chosen, internationally accepted reference standard used to measure and express a physical quantity.
A ratio of two equivalent quantities expressed in different units, which is mathematically equal to 1 and used for unit conversion.
A dimensionless vector of unit magnitude pointing in a specific direction, used to specify direction.
Work has the dimensional formula [ML^2T^-2] and is measured in the SI unit of Joules (J), with other common units including ergs, electron volts, calories, and kilowatt hours.
The units of Kc and Kp which depend on the stoichiometric coefficients of the reaction, or can be made dimensionless by referencing standard states.
The measurement scales for power, primarily the SI unit watt (W) and the engine unit horsepower (hp).
Standard and alternative units used to quantify pressure, including the atmosphere (atm), torr (equivalent to 1 mm Hg), and bar.
The dimensional units used to express reaction rates, typically concentration divided by time or pressure divided by time.
The specific units of the rate constant k, which vary depending on the overall reaction order and are derived from the rate law.
The proportionality constant, denoted by G, in Newton's law of gravitation that determines the strength of the gravitational force between masses.

The law stating that every body in the universe attracts every other body with a force directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Axons enclosed by Schwann cells that do not form a myelin sheath around the axon.
A wave in which the plane of vibration changes randomly in very short intervals of time while remaining perpendicular to the direction of propagation.
Hydrocarbons that contain carbon-carbon multiple bonds, which can be double bonds, triple bonds, or both.
Sequences of mRNA located at both the 5'-end and 3'-end that are not translated into protein but are essential for translation efficiency.
The movement of small amounts of urea from the collecting tubule back into the thin segment of the ascending limb of Henle's loop to help maintain medullary osmolarity.
A harmful condition characterized by the accumulation of urea in the blood due to kidney malfunction, which can lead to kidney failure.
The process of excreting urea, a less toxic nitrogenous waste produced in the liver from ammonia, allowing for water conservation in terrestrial animals like mammals.
The process of excreting nitrogenous wastes as uric acid in the form of a pellet or paste with minimal water loss, characteristic of reptiles, birds, and insects.
The physiological process of producing urine, which involves glomerular filtration, selective reabsorption, and tubular secretion.
The commercial and industrial applications of ethanoic acid, primarily as an industrial solvent and as vinegar in the food industry.
The specific industrial application of hexanedioic acid (adipic acid) in the synthesis and manufacture of the polymer nylon-6,6.
The commercial application of long-chain carboxylic acids (higher fatty acids) in the manufacturing of soaps and detergents.
The industrial applications of methanoic acid, which include its use in the rubber, textile, dyeing, leather, and electroplating industries.
The application of sodium benzoate, a salt of benzoic acid, as a preservative in the food industry to prevent spoilage.
A single, pear-shaped muscular organ where embryonic development occurs, composed of three tissue layers: perimetrium, myometrium, and endometrium.
A preventive process based on the immune system's memory, where weakened or inactivated pathogens are introduced to stimulate antibody and memory cell production.
The application of transgenic animals to test the safety of vaccines and evaluate the toxicity of drugs or chemical substances.
Membrane-bound spaces in the cytoplasm enclosed by the tonoplast, serving storage functions in plants and specialized roles like osmoregulation and excretion in protists.
Electronic devices that control the flow of electrons emitted from a heated cathode through a vacuum using electrodes.
The combining capacity of an atom, determined by its valence electrons, or the charge acquired by an atom based on electronegativity considerations.
The valence band is the energy band containing valence electrons, while the conduction band is the energy band directly above it where electrons can move freely to conduct electricity.
A quantum mechanical theory of chemical bonding that explains bond formation through the overlap of atomic orbitals and the pairing of electron spins.

canonical_label: Valence Bond Theory in Coordination Compounds
definition: A theory explaining bonding in coordination complexes where metal orbitals undergo hybridization to yield equivalent orbitals of definite geometry.
The electrons residing in the outermost shell of an atom, which has the highest principal quantum number.
The outer shell electrons that participate in chemical bonding, represented by simple dot notations around the element's symbol.

canonical_label: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) Theory

definition: The restriction of Hooke's law to only the linear region of the stress-strain relationship.
The constraint that standard kinematic equations are only applicable to one-dimensional motion where the acceleration remains constant in both magnitude and direction.
A mathematical factor introduced to account for the extent of solute dissociation or association in a solution.
The mole fractions of components in the vapour phase in equilibrium with a liquid solution, determined by their partial pressures and total pressure.
A class of mini-satellite DNA where a small sequence is repeated tandemly, showing high polymorphism in copy numbers among individuals.

canonical_label: Variable Oxidation States of Transition Metals
definition: The ability of transition elements to exhibit a wide variety of oxidation states in their compounds due to the incomplete filling of d orbitals.
The physical parameters (magnetic field, coil area, or orientation) whose alteration changes the magnetic flux and induces an emf.
The degree by which progeny differ from their parents.
The phenomenon where the conductivity of an electrolytic solution decreases upon dilution because the number of current-carrying ions per unit volume decreases.
The physical principle that the acceleration due to Earth's gravity is maximum at the surface and decreases with both height and depth.

canonical_label: Variation of Molar Conductivity with Dilution
definition: The increase in molar conductivity that occurs upon dilution because the total volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte increases.
An experiment using leaves with both green and non-green areas to demonstrate that photosynthesis occurs only in the chlorophyll-containing parts of the leaf.
Strands of vascular tissue consisting of xylem and phloem, classified into different types based on the presence of cambium and their relative arrangement.
The conducting system of plants composed of complex tissues, namely xylem and phloem, which are organized into vascular bundles.

manual_merge.reason: Manual high-precision merge: Vector == Vectors (plural_or_trivial_wording).

definition: The extension of analytical vector addition to three-dimensional space by summing the x, y, and z components of the vectors.
The rules governing the addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication of vector quantities.
The resolution of the force vector equation into independent component equations along orthogonal axes.
The mathematical representation of Coulomb's law using vectors to account for the directional nature (attraction or repulsion) of the electrostatic force.
An alternative formulation of Ohm's law expressing current density as the product of conductivity and the electric field vector.

canonical_label: Vector Magnitude and Direction from Components
definition: The calculation of a vector's magnitude and its angle of direction using its rectangular components.

A binary operation on two vectors in three-dimensional space that results in a vector perpendicular to both, with a magnitude equal to the product of their magnitudes and the sine of the angle between them.
The application of the cross product to orthogonal unit vectors, resulting in zero for parallel unit vectors and cyclic permutations of the unit vectors for orthogonal ones.
A physical quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction and obeys specific laws of addition, such as the triangle or parallelogram laws.
The process of splitting a single vector into two or more component vectors such that their sum equals the original vector.
The process of splitting a vector into constituent component vectors along specified coordinate axes using unit vectors.

canonical_label: Vector Scaling by a Negative Real Number
definition: Multiplying a vector by a negative real number scales its magnitude and reverses its direction.

canonical_label: Vector Scaling by a Positive Real Number
definition: Multiplying a vector by a positive real number scales its magnitude by that factor while keeping its direction unchanged.
The time-dependent rate of change of displacement and velocity for a particle undergoing simple harmonic motion.
The rate of change of position in a plane, resolved into x and y components, representing both average and instantaneous velocity.
The rate of change of displacement of an oscillating particle, which can be derived geometrically from a reference circle or by differentiating the displacement function with respect to time.

canonical_label: Velocity of the Centre of Mass
definition: The velocity of the centre of mass of a system of particles, obtained by differentiating the position relation with respect to time, where the total mass times this velocity equals the sum of the individual momenta.
The vector equation relating velocity, initial velocity, constant acceleration, and time in two dimensions, which can be resolved into independent component equations.
The arrangement of veins and veinlets in the lamina of a leaf.
The phases of ventricular contraction and relaxation that regulate blood flow to the pulmonary artery and aorta through pressure changes and valve actions.
A realistic model of population growth in an environment with limited resources, characterized by a sigmoid (S-shaped) curve that levels off as the population reaches carrying capacity.
A dorsally positioned structure formed by 26 serially arranged vertebrae that protects the spinal cord and supports the head.
A major subdivision of Chordata consisting of animals with a vertebral column, divided into jawless Agnatha and jawed Gnathostomata.
The motion of an object along a circular path in a vertical plane, where gravity and tension continuously change the object's speed and energy distribution.
Modes of motion involving internal oscillations of atoms in a molecule, contributing both kinetic and potential energy terms, resulting in an average energy of k_B T per mode.
Dihaloalkanes in which two halogen atoms of the same type are attached to adjacent carbon atoms.
Compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to an sp2-hybridised carbon atom of a carbon-carbon double bond.
Infectious agents smaller than viruses that consist of free, low molecular weight RNA and lack a protein coat.
Non-cellular obligate parasites characterized by an inert crystalline structure outside a living cell, containing genetic material (either DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat.
A distinct dorsal region of the mollusc body that contains the internal organs and is covered by a soft, spongy layer of skin called the mantle.
A part of the peripheral nervous system comprising nerves, fibres, ganglia, and plexuses that conduct impulses between the central nervous system and the viscera.
The internal resistance to motion between layers of fluid in relative motion.
A historical theory proposed by Berzelius suggesting that a mysterious force was necessary for the formation of organic compounds.
Specific health disorders, such as scurvy, rickets, or night blindness, caused by an insufficient intake of particular vitamins.
Organic compounds required in small amounts in the diet for normal growth, health, and specific biological functions.
The reproductive trait of giving birth to live young that have developed inside the body of the parent, which is characteristic of almost all mammals except for a few egg-laying species.
The angular deflection produced in the galvanometer coil per unit voltage applied across its terminals.
The amount of electric charge per unit volume in a three-dimensional space.
The principle that, for an incompressible fluid, the volume of fluid pushed down by the input piston must equal the volume of fluid pushed up by the output piston.
The volume of fluid flowing per unit time through a given cross-sectional area, represented by the product of area and velocity.
The volume of a component per 100 units of volume of the solution.
A model for predicting molecular geometry based on the minimization of electrostatic repulsion between electron pairs around a central atom.
The fundamental rules of VSEPR theory stating that molecular geometry is determined by minimizing electrostatic repulsions between all valence shell electron pairs surrounding the central atom.
An industrial process that utilizes palladium chloride ($PdCl_2$) as a catalyst to oxidize ethyne to ethanal.
Behavioral, academic, and psychological indicators in youth that suggest drug or alcohol abuse, such as dropping academic performance, isolation, and mood swings.
The demonstration by Cornelius van Niel that oxygen released during photosynthesis is derived from water, not carbon dioxide, through a light-dependent reaction.
A unique pathway of water transport in sponges where water enters through ostia into the spongocoel and exits through the osculum, assisting in food gathering, respiration, and excretion.
An essential requirement for plant growth that enables cell enlargement through turgidity and acts as a medium for enzymatic activities.
Vitamins (B group and vitamin C) that dissolve in water, cannot be stored in the body (except B12), and must be regularly supplied in the diet.
A protein complex associated with Photosystem II, located on the inner side of the thylakoid membrane, that facilitates the splitting of water.
The reaction occurring on the inner side of the thylakoid membrane that releases protons into the lumen, contributing to the proton gradient.
The closing of stomata triggered by water deficit, which reduces the availability of carbon dioxide for photosynthesis.
A distinctive hydraulic system in echinoderms used for locomotion, food capture and transport, and respiration.
The component of AC current that does not consume any power because it is out of phase with the voltage by ninety degrees.
A pattern of disturbance that propagates through a medium or space, transporting energy and information without the bulk flow of matter.
A mathematical function depending on electron coordinates that contains all information about the electron, though it has no direct physical meaning.
The phenomenon where two sinusoidal waves of the same frequency superpose, resulting in constructive or destructive interference depending on their phase difference.
The distinction between wave propagation, which involves local oscillations without net transport of the medium, and bulk motion, which involves the physical transport of matter.
The theory that electromagnetic radiation consists of oscillating electric and magnetic fields propagating through space as waves, which do not require a physical medium to travel.

canonical_label: Wave Nature of Macroscopic vs Microscopic Objects
definition: The concept explaining that wave properties are only experimentally detectable for subatomic particles due to their extremely small masses, unlike heavy macroscopic objects.

canonical_label: Wave Nature of Macroscopic vs Sub-atomic Objects
definition: The phenomenon where the wave character of macroscopic objects is undetectable due to extremely small wavelengths, whereas sub-atomic particles have measurable wavelengths comparable to atomic scales.
The period is the time for one complete oscillation of an element, while frequency and angular frequency describe the rate of these oscillations.
Amplitude is the maximum displacement from equilibrium, while phase is the argument of the sine function determining displacement at any position and time.
The classical model of light as an electromagnetic wave consisting of electric and magnetic fields with a continuous distribution of energy over space.
The rate at which a point of constant phase, such as a wave crest, propagates through space over time.
The principle that the speed of a mechanical wave depends solely on the elastic and inertial properties of the medium, independent of the source's velocity.
The mathematical expression of wave speed as the ratio of angular frequency to wave number, derived by keeping the phase of the wave constant.
The speed at which a wave propagates through a medium, determined by the medium's elastic and inertial properties.
A theory proposing that light propagates as a wave, which correctly predicted that the speed of light is slower in a denser medium, later confirmed experimentally by Foucault.
A surface of constant phase where all points of the medium oscillate in phase.
The modification of wavefront shapes (such as tilting or becoming spherical) as they pass through prisms, lenses, or reflect off mirrors due to varying propagation delays.
Wavelength is the minimum distance between two points in phase, and the angular wave number is the spatial frequency of the wave.
The distance between consecutive corresponding points of an electromagnetic wave, typically measured in meters or smaller units of length.
The number of wavelengths per unit length, mathematically defined as the reciprocal of the wavelength.
Substances that only partially dissociate into ions in aqueous solution, leaving most of the substance as unionized molecules.
The SI unit of magnetic flux, equivalent to one tesla meter squared (T m^2).
A convention for representing three-dimensional molecular structures on a two-dimensional surface using solid wedges, dashed wedges, and normal lines.
The phenomenon where objects in an orbiting satellite experience a state of weightlessness because they are in a continuous state of free fall.
A specific circuit network of four resistors used to determine an unknown resistance when balanced under a null condition.
An experimental circuit arrangement using an alternating current (AC) source and a detector to measure the unknown electrical resistance of an ionic solution.

An electrical circuit configuration consisting of four resistors arranged in a loop, with a voltage source connected across one diagonal pair of junctions and a galvanometer connected across the other.
The practical use of complete genome sequences to analyze all genes, transcripts, and protein networks within tissues, organs, or tumors.
A type of leaf arrangement where more than two leaves arise at a single node to form a circle or whorl.
A law stating that the wavelength of maximum emission intensity from a blackbody is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature.
A set of unpleasant and potentially severe symptoms, such as anxiety, shakiness, nausea, and sweating, that occur when the regular intake of drugs or alcohol is suddenly stopped.
The laboratory synthesis of the organic compound urea from the inorganic compound ammonium cyanate, which disproved the Vital Force Theory.
The product of the displacement and the component of the force along the direction of displacement.

canonical_label: Work Done by a Constant Force
definition: The work done on an object by a constant force is defined as the scalar product of the force vector and the displacement vector.
The work done on a rotating body by an applied torque over an angular displacement, mathematically expressed as dW = \tau d\theta.

canonical_label: Work Done by a Variable Force
definition: The total work done by a force that varies with position, calculated by summing infinitesimal work intervals or integrating the force over the displacement interval.

canonical_label: Work Done by an Expanding Gas
definition: The work performed by a gas when it expands against a constant external pressure, calculated as the product of pressure and the change in volume.
The principle that magnetic force does no work on a moving charged particle because the force is always perpendicular to the velocity.

canonical_label: Work Done in an Electric Field
Work Done in an Electrostatic Field
definition: The integral of the external force required to counter the electrostatic force when moving a charge along a path.
A theorem stating that the change in kinetic energy of a particle is equal to the net work done on it by the forces acting upon it.
The Work-Energy theorem represents a scalar formulation of Newton's Second Law and remains valid across all inertial reference frames.
An extension of the work-energy theorem stating that the change in total mechanical energy of a system equals the work done by non-conservative forces.
The minimum energy required to eject an electron from the surface of a metal.
The precise physical quantity contrasted with everyday activities like studying or painting.
A coupling reaction where alkyl halides are treated with sodium metal in dry ether to yield higher alkanes containing an even number of carbon atoms.
A sex chromosome identified initially as the 'X body' by Henking during cytological observations of spermatogenesis in insects.
High-energy electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths shorter than ultraviolet rays, commonly generated by bombarding metal targets with high-energy electrons and used in medical imaging.
The transfer of pollen grains from the anther of a flower on one plant to the stigma of a flower on a genetically different plant.
A mechanism of sex determination where females have a pair of X-chromosomes (XX) and males have only one X-chromosome (XO).
A sex determination system where females have a pair of X-chromosomes (XX) and males have one X and one Y-chromosome (XY).
The mechanism where human sex is determined by sex chromosomes, with females being XX and males being XY.
The point on the stress-strain curve, also known as the elastic limit, beyond which the material undergoes plastic deformation and does not return to its original dimensions when the load is removed.
An experimental setup that splits a single light source into two coherent wavefronts using closely spaced slits to demonstrate light interference.
The ratio of tensile or compressive stress to longitudinal strain for an object under tension or compression.
The phenomenon of the splitting of spectral lines in the presence of a static magnetic field.
A chemical reaction in which the rate of reaction is independent of the concentration of the reactants, meaning the rate is proportional to the zero power of reactant concentration.
The physical condition where no work is performed because either the displacement is zero, the force is zero, or the force and displacement vectors are mutually perpendicular.
The law stating that if two thermodynamic systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.
A catalytic system composed of titanium tetrachloride ($TiCl_4$) and triethylaluminium ($Al(CH_3)_3$) used in the polymerization of ethylene to manufacture polyethylene.
The dipolar ionic structure formed by sulphanilic acid (p-aminobenzene sulphonic acid) due to internal proton transfer from the acidic sulphonic group to the basic amino group.
The dipolar ionic form of an amino acid where the molecule carries both a positive and a negative charge simultaneously, varying with the pH of the solution.
An ART procedure where a zygote or early embryo with up to 8 blastomeres is transferred into the fallopian tube.
